scenarios.title: Data Exfiltration
scenarios.description: An employee is suspected of unauthorized copying of sensitive data (code, trade secrets, etc) from internal systems to those outside of the company's control.

questions.facet_name: Are there any indications of anti-forensic efforts?
questions.text: Was an Incognito/Private browser session used?

approaches.name: Search CrowdStrike logs for Incognito Chrome processes
approaches.description:
CrowdStrike records the source process ID (ContextProcessId) for ProcessRollup events.
Crowdstrike is a detection platform, not a logging platform, so not all executions are logged. We cannot always connect a running browser process with observed DNS requests. When we do see DNS requests coming from a browser process, yet we don't see browsing history, there are several possible explanations, including browser extensions or private browsing.

questions.facet_name: Are there any indications of anti-forensic efforts?
questions.text: Was there specialized "anti-forensic" (or "privacy") software on a computer?

questions.facet_name: Are there any indications of anti-forensic efforts?
questions.text: Were any encryption programs used on a computer?

questions.facet_name: Are there any indications of anti-forensic efforts?
questions.text: Were any security or monitoring agents tampered with or disabled?

questions.facet_name: Are there any indications of anti-forensic efforts?
questions.text: What actions were taken in an Incognito (or "private") browser session?

questions.facet_name: Are there any indications of anti-forensic efforts?
questions.text: Was shell history cleared?

questions.facet_name: Are there any indications of anti-forensic efforts?
questions.text: Were any system event logs cleared?

approaches.name: Examine Windows Event Logs for Audit Log cleared
approaches.description:
Parse the Windows Security Event Log and look for "the audit log was cleared" event.
On Windows systems, log clearance events for Security event log will be logged with event ID 1102. The logs contain the actor account name, domain name, logon id fields.
refs: [Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs on MITRE ATT&CK](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1070/001/)

approaches.name: Search CrowdStrike logs for indicator removal on host
approaches.description:
CrowdStrike has built-in detections for "indicator removal on host" events.
Clearance/deletion of system logs would be recorded in CrowdStrike as a part of their "indicator removal on host" detections.

questions.facet_name: Are there signs of staging data for future exfiltration?
questions.text: What files were downloaded using a web browser?

approaches.name: Detect browser downloads via change journal records
approaches.description:
File downloads by some web browsers create a specific pattern of events on the file system. We can use this to see browser downloads using NTFS change journal (USN journal) records.
Chrome downloads on Windows create a specific pattern of events on the file system:
1.  **Download starts with a `FILE CREATE` event to `.tmp` file; older Chrome versions
    have been observed creating four character file names (like `7DEE.tmp`)
    while newer versions use file names consistent with Version 4 UUIDs
    (like `183011a0-f96c-4c98-b71b-ab8754fb0e0e.tmp`).
1.  That `.tmp` file might be `RENAME`d to `Unconfirmed [6 digits].crdownload`. 1.  If [2] `Unconfirmed...crdownload` `RENAME` didn't happen, the `.tmp` file is
    renamed to the eventual filename (with extension) with `.crdownload`
appended
    at the end (like `cat.jpg.crdownload`). **In either case ([2] or [3]),
at
    this point we have a file ending in .crdownload.**
1.  Once the download completes, the `.crdownload` suffix is removed via a
    `USN_REASON_RENAME_OLD_NAME` event, leaving the downloaded file with its
final name.
Since [4] above captures the key information about the file download, we can search change journal records for it.

approaches.name: Detect browser downloads via file system event logs
approaches.description:
File downloads by some web browsers create a specific pattern of events on the file system. We can use this to see browser downloads using file system logs (like Santa).
Chrome downloads on macOS create a specific pattern of file system events:
1.  **Download starts with a `WRITE` event to a temp file named like
    `.com.google.Chrome.avXx0U`** (the suffix looks to be 6
letters/numbers). 1.  That .com.google.Chrome... temp file might be `RENAME`d to `Unconfirmed [6
    digits].crdownload`.
1.  If [2] `Unconfirmed...crdownload` `RENAME` didn't happen, the .com.google.
    temp file is renamed to the eventual filename (with extension) with
    `.crdownload` appended at the end (like `cat.jpg.crdownload`). **In
either
    case ([2] or [3]), at this point we have a file ending in .crdownload.**
1.  Once the download completes, the `.crdownload` suffix is removed via a
    `RENAME` event, leaving the downloaded file with its final name.

Since [4] above captures the key information about the file download, we can search file system logs for it.

approaches.name: Collect download records from local browser artifacts (Plaso)
approaches.description:
Parse download records from web browsers' own databases.
Most web browsers track file downloads (at least in non-Incognito/private sessions) in local artifacts on the system. We can collect and review these records. This approach is useful when you have remote access to a system (via GRR for example) or a disk image.

approaches.name: Collect download records from local browser artifacts (Hindsight)
approaches.description:
Parse download records from web browsers' own databases.
Most web browsers track file downloads (at least in non-Incognito/private sessions) in local artifacts on the system. We can collect and review these records. This approach is useful when you have remote access to a system (via GRR for example) or a disk image.

scenarios.title: Data Infiltration
scenarios.description: An employee is suspected of taking confidential information or trade secrets from a prior employer and introducing it to internal systems.

scenarios.title: Suspicious DNS Query
scenarios.description: A DNS query to an unexpected domain can be an indicator of abnormal activity on a host. If a domain has been marked as malicious, an investigator may be tasked with determining what caused the DNS query (or response) and if it indicates the host has been compromised.

questions.facet_name: What application was responsible for the DNS query?
questions.text: What process made the DNS query?

approaches.name: Use Crowdstrike event search to link source processes to DNS queries
approaches.description:
CrowdStrike records the source process ID (ContextProcessId) for DNSRequest event.
Crowdstrike is a detection platform, not a logging platform, so not all DNS requests are logged. Content Filter needs to be enabled to capture DNS request queries.

approaches.name: Use Crowdstrike "Bulk Domains" to link source processes to DNS queries
approaches.description:
CrowdStrike records the source process ID (ContextProcessId) for DNSRequest event.
Crowdstrike is a detection platform, not a logging platform, so not all DNS requests are logged. Content Filter needs to be enabled to capture DNS request queries.

approaches.name: Use Sysmon (Event ID 22) to link source processes to DNS queries (Splunk)
approaches.description:
Sysmon Event ID 22 DnsQuery stores source process ID
DNS Query, event ID 22, records a DNS query being issued by a specific host and the originating process.

approaches.name: Use Sysmon (Event ID 22) to link source processes to DNS queries (Plaso)
approaches.description:
Sysmon Event ID 22 DnsQuery stores source process ID
DNS Query, event ID 22, records a DNS query being issued by a specific host and the originating process.

questions.facet_name: What application was responsible for the DNS query?
questions.text: Have there been any modifications to the "hosts" file?

questions.facet_name: Was a user's web browsing the cause of a given DNS query?
questions.text: What web browsers were running at a given time?

approaches.name: Collect process executions in Crowdstrike event search
approaches.description:
CrowdStrike records process executions in ProcessRollup event.
CrowdStrike is a detection platform, not a logging platform, so not all ProcessRollup events might be logged.

questions.facet_name: Was a user's web browsing the cause of a given DNS query?
questions.text: What pages did web browsers visit?

questions.text: Was an Incognito/Private browser session used?

Crowdstrike is a detection platform, not a logging platform, so not all executions are logged. We cannot always connect a running browser process with observed DNS requests. When we do see DNS requests coming from a browser process, yet we don't see browsing history, there are several possible explanations, including browser extensions or private browsing.

scenarios.title: Cloud Project Compromise Assessment
scenarios.description: Evaluate a cloud project for malicious or unknown activities and determine if the cloud project is compromised. This is distinct from evaluating whether a particular instance in that cloud project is compromised.

questions.facet_name: Has an attacker gained access to the cloud project? (Initial Access)
questions.text: Are there any detections of exposed service account credentials?

questions.facet_name: Has an attacker gained access to the cloud project? (Initial Access)
questions.text: Are there any interaction with the cloud project resources from an unknown external IP address (including Tor Exit nodes, C2 tagged addresses)?

questions.facet_name: Has an attacker gained access to the cloud project? (Initial Access)
questions.text: Are there any unexpected successful API calls from an unknown external IP address (including Tor Exit nodes, C2 tagged addresses)?

questions.facet_name: Are there any signs of an attacker trying to maintain access to the cloud project? (Persistence)
questions.text: Are there any APIs recently enabled?

questions.facet_name: Are there any signs of an attacker trying to maintain access to the cloud project? (Persistence)
questions.text: Are there any role bindings to cluster admin for anonymous users?

questions.facet_name: Are there any signs of an attacker trying to maintain access to the cloud project? (Persistence)
questions.text: Are there any unexpected resources created under the cloud project?

questions.facet_name: Are there any signs of an attacker trying to maintain access to the cloud project? (Persistence)
questions.text: Are there any unknown accounts created?

questions.facet_name: Are there any signs of an attacker trying to maintain access to the cloud project? (Persistence)
questions.text: Are there any unknown role bindings created?

questions.facet_name: Are there any signs of interference with cloud project protective measures? (Defense Evasion)
questions.text: Are there any suspicious modifications to the cloud project firewall rules/policies?

questions.facet_name: Are there any signs of interference with cloud project protective measures? (Defense Evasion)
questions.text: Are there any suspicious modifications to the cloud project logging configuration?

questions.facet_name: Are there indications of an attacker moving between resources?
questions.text: Is there an unusual increase of traffic between resource in the cloud project?

questions.facet_name: Are there any signs of data exfiltration from resources inside the cloud project? (Data Exfil)
questions.text: Are there any GCS buckets within the cloud project shared externally?

questions.facet_name: Are there any signs of data exfiltration from resources inside the cloud project? (Data Exfil)
questions.text: Are there any signs of data within the cloud project being transferred externally?

questions.facet_name: Are there any detections of potentially suspicious activity related to the cloud project from detection tools?
questions.text: Are there any detections of exposed service account credentials?

scenarios.title: Host Persistence Audit
scenarios.description: Once an attacker is on a system, a main goal becomes trying to maintain that access. Persistence mechanisms give the attacker the ability to run malware that will survive reboots and credential changes.

questions.text: What Launch Daemons are configured?

questions.text: What login hooks are configured?

questions.text: What network logon scripts are configured?

questions.text: What local accounts have been created?

questions.text: What Scheduled Tasks are configured?

questions.text: Are there any indications of dylib hijacking?

questions.text: Are there any indications of dylib proxying?

scenarios.title: Lateral Movement
scenarios.description: Lateral movement is the process of an attacker moving from one system to another within a network after they have gained initial access to a system. This can be done through a variety of methods, such as exploiting vulnerabilities in software, using stolen credentials, installing their own remote access tools, or using native system utilities.

questions.facet_name: Are there any new accounts that have been created?
questions.text: What domain accounts have been created?

questions.facet_name: Are there any new accounts that have been created?
questions.text: What local accounts have been created?

questions.facet_name: Are the indications of an attacker moving FROM this host to another?
questions.text: Have there been any executions of PsExec?

approaches.name: Examine Windows Event Logs for PsExec executions
approaches.description:
Parse the Windows Security Event Log and look for PsExec processes being created.
Windows systems can be set to log new process creation events to the Security event log. The logs contain the new process name, process ID, parent process name, process creation time, and many more useful fields.

approaches.name: Examine Windows Prefetch for evidence of PsExec usage
approaches.description:
Parse Windows Prefetch files and look for PsExec.
Windows Prefetch files are designed to speed up application start times. To do this, they store information about recently launched applications, including the file name of the executable and the number of times it has been run.

questions.facet_name: Are the indications of an attacker moving TO this host?
questions.text: Have there been any executions of PsExeSrv?

approaches.name: Examine Windows Event Logs for PsExeSvc Service Installations
approaches.description:
Parse the Windows Security Event Log and look for the PsExeSvc service being installed.
Windows systems can be set to log new service installations to the System event log. The logs contain the new service name, service file name, and more. When used to access a remote system, PsExec installs a service (by default named `PSEXESVC`) on the target system.

approaches.name: Examine Windows Prefetch for evidence of PsExeSvc
approaches.description:
Parse Windows Prefetch files and look for PsExeSvc.
Windows Prefetch files are designed to speed up application start times. To do this, they store information about recently launched applications, including the file name of the executable and the number of times it has been run.

approaches.name: Examine Windows Event Logs for PsExeSvc executions
approaches.description:
Parse the Windows Security Event Log and look for PsExeSvc processes being created.
Windows systems can be set to log new process creation events to the Security event log. The logs contain the new process name, process ID, parent process name, process creation time, and many more useful fields.
